name: Myrtle Aphrodite
description: Myrtle, particularly the variety associated with Aphrodite, holds a prominent place in Greek mythology and culture. This evergreen shrub, known for its fragrant leaves and delicate white flowers, was considered sacred to the goddess of love, beauty, and fertility. Legend has it that Aphrodite used myrtle to conceal her nakedness after her birth from the sea, solidifying its connection to her essence. It symbolizes love, beauty, immortality, and good luck. Myrtle was frequently used in rituals and ceremonies dedicated to Aphrodite, adorning her statues and altars. Brides wore myrtle wreaths as a symbol of love and fidelity, and it was also used in funerary rites, representing eternal life. The plant's association with Aphrodite extended to its perceived aphrodisiac properties, making it a popular ingredient in love potions and perfumes. Beyond its religious and romantic significance, myrtle was also valued for its medicinal properties, believed to possess antiseptic and anti-inflammatory qualities. Its leaves and berries were used in traditional remedies to treat various ailments, further solidifying its importance in ancient Greek society. Myrtle's enduring presence in Greek art, literature, and folklore attests to its deep-rooted cultural significance as a symbol of love, beauty, and immortality.
subtitle: Aphrodite's sacred symbol of love.

keyMyths.title: Aphrodite's Birth
keyMyths.description: After Aphrodite was born from the sea foam, she used myrtle branches to cover her nakedness. This act solidified the plant's association with her beauty and love.

keyMyths.title: Sacrifices to Aphrodite
keyMyths.description: Myrtle was a common offering to Aphrodite in temples and during festivals dedicated to her. Burning myrtle incense was believed to please the goddess and invoke her blessings.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Describes the birth of Aphrodite and her connection to myrtle.

sources.author: Pausanias
